summary:
Iontophoresis typically uses strong fluoride solutions due to its deep penetration capability; thus, options like 2%, which are commonly found in professional dental therapies aimed at treating cavities or enhancing enamel health through intense topical application, seem most fitting based on standard practice guidelines.
To address your requirements more succinctly while retaining essential elements,
Key Points Summarized: For effective treatment via iontophoresis, concentrated fluorides are utilized—common choices include approximately 2%, reflecting their use in intensive preventive dentistry procedures targeting deeper tissue levels compared to everyday toothpaste concentrations. This reflects both efficacy needs and practical considerations regarding drug absorption mechanisms during therapy administration. To directly answer within two concise statements focusing only on critical aspects related to the query posed by the user:

Iontophoretic fluoride treatments employ highly concentrated forms suitable for penetrating skin layers effectively; hence, using approximations derived from well-established clinical protocols indicates an optimal choice near 2%. Such concentrations ensure sufficient contact time and depth reach necessary for significant benefits in preventing decay or promoting re-mineralization processes targeted under these specialized conditions. Therefore, \(\boxed{\text{Option B}}\) accurately represents recommended dosages consistent across various authoritative sources discussing advanced fluoride delivery systems including those utilizing electrical currents facilitated by iontophoresis technology.
